rag.description: The SetFocus action programmatically places keyboard focus on a target web element by executing the JavaScript focus() method via the W3C Execute Script API. It fires onfocus and focusin DOM events and is used to activate input fields, trigger event-driven UI behaviors, and verify programmatic focusability. When the target element cannot be located the action exits silently without raising an exception.

qa.question: What does the SetFocus action do?
qa.answer: SetFocus executes the JavaScript focus() method on the target element, placing keyboard focus on it without simulating a mouse click.

qa.question: How is focus set on the element?
qa.answer: The action calls InvokeScript with the script 'arguments[0].focus()' and passes the located element as the argument, using the W3C POST /session/{session id}/execute/sync endpoint.

qa.question: What happens when the target element is not found?
qa.answer: When the element cannot be located, the action returns an empty plugin response and does not throw an exception.

qa.question: What focus-related events does SetFocus trigger?
qa.answer: Calling focus() on an element fires the onfocus and focusin DOM events, which can activate validation handlers, autocomplete dropdowns, or visual effects bound to those events.

qa.question: Where is the source code for SetFocus?
What W3C WebDriver endpoint does SetFocus use?
qa.answer: SetFocus uses the W3C WebDriver POST /session/{session id}/execute/sync endpoint to execute the JavaScript arguments[0].focus() call.

qa.question: What is the manifest version for SetFocus?
What locator strategies does SetFocus support?
qa.answer: SetFocus supports Xpath (default), CssSelector, Id, LinkText, and PartialLinkText via the Locator property.

description: ### Purpose
The SetFocus action programmatically places keyboard focus on a target web element by executing the JavaScript `focus()` method via the W3C Execute Script API.
It is used to activate input fields and interactive controls before subsequent keyboard interactions, and to trigger focus-related DOM events such as `onfocus` and `focusin`.
When the target element cannot be located the action exits silently without raising an exception.
### Key Features and Functionality
| Feature            | Description                                                                              |
| JavaScript Focus   | Calls `arguments[0].focus()` on the located element via the W3C Execute Script API.      |
| Event Triggering   | Fires `onfocus` and `focusin` DOM events, activating any handlers bound to those events. |
| Graceful No-Op     | Returns an empty response without throwing when the target element is not found.         |
| Locator Strategies | Supports Xpath, CssSelector, Id, LinkText, and PartialLinkText for element targeting.    |
### Usages in RPA
| Use Case              | Description                                                                                     |
| Form Field Activation | Focuses an input field before typed or scripted text entry to ensure keystrokes land correctly. |
| Event-Driven Workflow | Triggers onfocus handlers that load dynamic content, suggestions, or validation feedback.       |
| Multi-Step Forms      | Moves focus to the next field in sequence to simulate natural tab-order navigation.             |
### Usages in Automation Testing
| Use Case                 | Description                                                                                                    |
| Focus Event Verification | Confirms that onfocus and focusin handlers fire and produce expected UI changes when an element is focused.    |
| Input Readiness Testing  | Verifies that an input element is reachable and focusable before asserting keyboard interaction behavior.      |
| Accessibility Compliance | Tests that interactive elements are programmatically focusable, meeting WCAG keyboard navigation requirements. |

edge_cases: Element is not in the DOM at the time of execution — action returns empty response silently
Element is hidden or has display:none — focus() call may silently fail in some browsers
Element is disabled — disabled inputs cannot receive focus and the browser ignores the call
Element is inside an iframe — the WebDriver must be switched to the correct frame before focusing
Element is inside a shadow DOM — standard locator may not reach it without explicit shadow-root traversal
Stale element reference after dynamic re-render — GetElement resolves a fresh reference each call
Multiple elements match the XPath — GetElement resolves the first DOM-order match; ensure the expression is specific
onfocus handler triggers navigation — subsequent actions execute after the handler completes
Element already has focus — calling focus() again fires focusin but causes no visible state change
annotations.expected_result: The input element identified by the XPath expression receives keyboard focus and the onfocus DOM event fires.
annotations.notes: Xpath is the default locator strategy. When onElement is an XPath expression and no explicit locator is set, the engine defaults to Xpath automatically.

description: ### Focus an input field using XPath
Locates the element matching `//input[@name='email']` using the default Xpath strategy and calls `focus()` on it.
Use this form when an XPath expression uniquely identifies the target input and no explicit locator override is needed.

edge_cases: CSS selector matches multiple elements — the first match in DOM order receives focus
Element is dynamically injected and not yet in the DOM — action returns empty response silently
Element is read-only — read-only inputs can receive focus and trigger onfocus events normally
onfocus handler modifies page layout — subsequent selectors may need to account for the layout change
Focus triggers an autocomplete dropdown — downstream actions should handle or dismiss the dropdown before proceeding
Browser tab is in the background — some browsers suppress focus events for background tabs
Element is covered by a sticky header or overlay — focus is set correctly but subsequent click interactions may be blocked
CSS selector includes a pseudo-class that does not resolve at runtime — no element found, action exits silently
annotations.expected_result: The element matching the CSS selector receives keyboard focus, activating any bound onfocus or focusin handlers.
annotations.notes: CssSelector is set explicitly because the onElement value is a CSS selector expression. Without the explicit locator the engine would attempt to parse the value as XPath.

description: ### Focus an element using a CSS selector
Locates the element matching `#username` using the CssSelector strategy and calls `focus()` on it.
Use this form when targeting an element by its ID or CSS expression is more convenient or reliable than an XPath.
Locator specifies the strategy used to find the target element.
Accepted values include Xpath, CssSelector, Id, LinkText, and PartialLinkText.
When absent the default Xpath strategy is used.
properties.name: Locator

description: OnElement provides the locator expression that identifies the element to receive focus.
It is evaluated using the strategy defined by the Locator property.
When the element cannot be located the action exits without throwing an exception.
properties.name: OnElement

summary: SetFocus places keyboard focus on a target web element by executing the JavaScript `focus()` method via the W3C Execute Script API.

It fires `onfocus` and `focusin` DOM events and is used to activate input fields before typed interaction, trigger event-driven UI behaviors, and verify programmatic focusability in automation testing.
When the target element cannot be located the action exits silently without raising an exception.
